We additionally demonstrate that the proposed AE-based scheme allows reliable communication when the channel is unknown or changing. Finally, we show that for multiple access the system can account for different user priorities. In summary, the proposed AE-based scheme enables end-to-end system optimization in complex scenarios unsuitable for analytical treatment and thereby brings molecular communication systems closer to real-world deployment.

</details>


### [326] [Index-Based Scheduling for a Resource-Constrained Quantum Switch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23476)
*Subhankar Banerjee,Stavros Mitrolaris,Sennur Ulukus*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Quantum switch scheduling for multipartite entanglement requests using restless multi-armed bandit framework with Whittle index policy and knapsack optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently serve multipartite entanglement requests among N users using a quantum switch with finite memory registers, optimizing the average number of requests served per unit time.

Method: Formulate scheduling as restless multi-armed bandit (RMAB) problem, prove indexability, derive closed-form Whittle index expressions, then solve memory-constrained scheduling as 0-1 knapsack problem using dynamic programming, plus two low-complexity greedy policies with modified Whittle indices.

Result: Showed entanglement request scheduling is indexable, derived closed-form Whittle index for all request-age pairs, developed optimal dynamic programming solution and two efficient greedy policies for memory-constrained scheduling.

Conclusion: The proposed RMAB framework with Whittle index provides effective scheduling policies for quantum switches with finite memory, balancing entanglement request service with memory constraints through both optimal and low-complexity solutions.

Abstract: We consider a quantum switch with a finite number of quantum memory registers that aims to serve multipartite entanglement requests among $N$ users. We propose scheduling policies that aim to optimize the average number of requests served per unit time by efficiently utilizing the switch's available memory. To measure the performance of the scheduling policies, we employ the newly introduced metric of age of entanglement establishment (AoEE). We formulate the scheduling problem in a restless multi-armed bandit (RMAB) framework. We show that the scheduling of entanglement requests is indexable. Subsequently, we find a closed-form expression of the Whittle index for all possible request-age pairs. By modeling the Whittle index of each request as its reward and its cardinality as its cost, we formulate the memory-constrained scheduling problem as a $0$-$1$ knapsack problem and solve it via dynamic programming. Furthermore, we consider two low-complexity sequential greedy policies that leverage two different modified Whittle indices.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [327] [CaP-X: A Framework for Benchmarking and Improving Coding Agents for Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22435)
*Max Fu,Justin Yu,Karim El-Refai,Ethan Kou,Haoru Xue,Huang Huang,Wenli Xiao,Guanzhi Wang,Fei-Fei Li,Guanya Shi,Jiajun Wu,Shankar Sastry,Yuke Zhu,Ken Goldberg,Linxi "Jim" Fan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CaP-X is an open-access framework for studying Code-as-Policy agents in robot manipulation, revealing that performance improves with human abstractions but degrades without them, though this gap can be mitigated through agentic test-time computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically explore how executable code can complement data-intensive Vision-Language-Action methods for embodied manipulation, as the effectiveness of Code-as-Policy agents as autonomous controllers remains underexplored.

Method: Developed CaP-X framework with CaP-Gym (interactive environment for program synthesis/execution) and CaP-Bench (evaluates language/VL models across abstraction levels). Introduced CaP-Agent0 (training-free framework) and CaP-RL (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards).

Result: Across 12 models, performance improves with human-crafted abstractions but degrades without them, showing dependence on designer scaffolding. However, agentic test-time computation (multi-turn interaction, feedback, visual differencing, skill synthesis, ensembled reasoning) mitigates this gap and improves robustness.

Conclusion: CaP-X provides a principled, open-access platform for advancing embodied coding agents, with CaP-Agent0 achieving human-level reliability on manipulation tasks and CaP-RL enabling sim2real transfer with minimal gap.

Abstract: "Code-as-Policy" considers how executable code can complement data-intensive Vision-Language-Action (VLA) methods, yet their effectiveness as autonomous controllers for embodied manipulation remains underexplored. We present CaP-X, an open-access framework for systematically studying Code-as-Policy agents in robot manipulation. At its core is CaP-Gym, an interactive environment in which agents control robots by synthesizing and executing programs that compose perception and control primitives. Building on this foundation, CaP-Bench evaluates frontier language and vision-language models across varying levels of abstraction, interaction, and perceptual grounding. Across 12 models, CaP-Bench reveals a consistent trend: performance improves with human-crafted abstractions but degrades as these priors are removed, exposing a dependence on designer scaffolding. At the same time, we observe that this gap can be mitigated through scaling agentic test-time computation--through multi-turn interaction, structured execution feedback, visual differencing, automatic skill synthesis, and ensembled reasoning--substantially improves robustness even when agents operate over low-level primitives. These findings allow us to derive CaP-Agent0, a training-free framework that recovers human-level reliability on several manipulation tasks in simulation and on real embodiments. We further introduce CaP-RL, showing reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves success rates and transfers from sim2real with minimal gap. Together, CaP-X provides a principled, open-access platform for advancing embodied coding agents.

</details>


### [328] [Wake Up to the Past: Using Memory to Model Fluid Wake Effects on Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22472)
*Luca Vendruscolo,Eduardo Sebastián,Amanda Prorok,Ajay Shankar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Data-driven wake-effect prediction for aerial/aquatic robots requires models with history support and transport delay prediction to accurately capture fluid-mediated interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wake effects between robots in fluid media are hard to model due to chaotic dynamics, complex geometries, and motion patterns. Current data-driven approaches using memory-less functions perform poorly in agile scenarios because they don't account for the finite propagation time of wake effects.

Method: Empirical study of seven data-driven models designed to capture spatio-temporal evolution of fluid wake effects in four different media. Models are introspected to analyze features enabling improved accuracy. Experimental validation using a planar rectilinear gantry for two spinning monocopters with real-world data and feedback control.

Result: Models that support history of previous states as input and incorporate transport delay prediction substantially improve accuracy in wake-effect prediction across different predictors and fluid media.

Conclusion: Accurate wake-effect prediction for fluid-mediated robot interactions requires models that account for historical state information and transport delays, rather than relying on memory-less current-state mappings.

Abstract: Autonomous aerial and aquatic robots that attain mobility by perturbing their medium, such as multicopters and torpedoes, produce wake effects that act as disturbances for adjacent robots. Wake effects are hard to model and predict due to the chaotic spatio-temporal dynamics of the fluid, entangled with the physical geometry of the robots and their complex motion patterns. Data-driven approaches using neural networks typically learn a memory-less function that maps the current states of the two robots to a force observed by the "sufferer" robot. Such models often perform poorly in agile scenarios: since the wake effect has a finite propagation time, the disturbance observed by a sufferer robot is some function of relative states in the past. In this work, we present an empirical study of the properties a wake-effect predictor must satisfy to accurately model the interactions between two robots mediated by a fluid. We explore seven data-driven models designed to capture the spatio-temporal evolution of fluid wake effects in four different media. This allows us to introspect the models and analyze the reasons why certain features enable improved accuracy in prediction across predictors and fluids. As experimental validation, we develop a planar rectilinear gantry for two spinning monocopters to test in real-world data with feedback control. The conclusion is that support of history of previous states as input and transport delay prediction substantially helps to learn an accurate wake-effect predictor.

</details>


### [329] [MapForest: A Modular Field Robotics System for Forest Mapping and Invasive Species Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22502)
*Sandeep Zachariah,Francisco Yandun,Sachet Korada,Abhisesh Silwal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MapForest: A modular field robotics system using multi-modal sensors to create GIS-ready invasive species maps for large forest areas with degraded GNSS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monitoring invasive tree species across large forests is challenging due to limited accessibility, manual scouting requirements, and poor GNSS under tree canopies.

Method: System includes: (1) compact platform-agnostic sensing payload for UAV/bicycle/backpack, (2) software pipeline with LiDAR-inertial mapping enhanced with covariance-aware GNSS factors, (3) object detector for Tree-of-Heaven from RGB imagery, and (4) fusion of detections with reconstructed maps.

Result: Mapping module achieved 1.95 m trajectory error over 1.2 km forest traversal; Tree-of-Heaven detector achieved F1 score of 0.653; datasets and tooling released for reproducible research.

Conclusion: MapForest provides an effective solution for invasive species monitoring in GNSS-challenged environments, with modular design supporting multiple deployment platforms and releasing datasets to advance forest mapping research.

Abstract: Monitoring and controlling invasive tree species across large forests, parks, and trail networks is challenging due to limited accessibility, reliance on manual scouting, and degraded under-canopy GNSS. We present MapForest, a modular field robotics system that transforms multi-modal sensor data into GIS-ready invasive-species maps. Our system features: (i) a compact, platform-agnostic sensing payload that can be rapidly mounted on UAV, bicycle, or backpack platforms, and (ii) a software pipeline comprising LiDAR-inertial mapping, image-based invasive-species detection, and georeferenced map generation. To ensure reliable operation in GNSS-intermittent environments, we enhance a LiDAR-inertial mapping backbone with covariance-aware GNSS factors and robust loss kernels. We train an object detector to detect the Tree-of-Heaven (Ailanthus altissima) from onboard RGB imagery and fuse detections with the reconstructed map to produce geospatial outputs suitable for downstream decision making. We collected a dataset spanning six sites across urban environments, parks, trails, and forests to evaluate individual system modules, and report end-to-end results on two sites containing Tree-of-Heaven. The enhanced mapping module achieved a trajectory deviation error of 1.95 m over a 1.2 km forest traversal, and the Tree-of-Heaven detector achieved an F1 score of 0.653. The datasets and associated tooling are released to support reproducible research in forest mapping and invasive-species monitoring.

</details>


### [330] [Energy-Aware Collaborative Exploration for a UAV-UGV Team](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22507)
*Cahit Ikbal Er,Saikiran Juttu,Yasin Yazicioglu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Energy-aware collaborative exploration framework for UAV-UGV teams with UAV flight-time limits, using coupled orienteering problems over layered roadmaps to maximize information gain while ensuring rendezvous for charging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAVs have limited flight time due to energy constraints, which restricts their exploration capabilities. By collaborating with a UGV that serves as a mobile charging station, the team can extend operational time while exploring unknown environments efficiently.

Method: UAV executes energy-bounded exploration tours while UGV explores on ground and serves as charging station. Uses density-aware layered probabilistic roadmap (PRM) to create sparsely coupled air-ground roadmap. Formulates tour selection as coupled orienteering problems (OPs) to maximize information gain subject to rendezvous constraints before UAV reaches flight-time limit.

Result: Method validated through simulation studies, benchmark comparisons, and real-world experiments, demonstrating effective collaborative exploration with energy constraints.

Conclusion: The framework enables efficient collaborative exploration of unknown environments by UAV-UGV teams while respecting UAV energy constraints through systematic rendezvous planning and coupled optimization.

Abstract: We present an energy-aware collaborative exploration framework for a UAV-UGV team operating in unknown environments, where the UAV's energy constraint is modeled as a maximum flight-time limit. The UAV executes a sequence of energy-bounded exploration tours, while the UGV simultaneously explores on the ground and serves as a mobile charging station. Rendezvous is enforced under a shared time budget so that the vehicles meet at the end of each tour before the UAV reaches its flight-time limit. We construct a sparsely coupled air-ground roadmap using a density-aware layered probabilistic roadmap (PRM) and formulate tour selection over the roadmap as coupled orienteering problems (OPs) to maximize information gain subject to the rendezvous constraint. The resulting tours are constructed over collision-validated roadmap edges. We validate our method through simulation studies, benchmark comparisons, and real-world experiments.

</details>


### [331] [Parallel OctoMapping: A Scalable Framework for Enhanced Path Planning in Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22508)
*Yihui Mao,Tian Tan,Xuehui Shen,Warren E. Dixon,Rushikesh Kamalapurkar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: POMP is a parallel OctoMap-based mapping method that refines free space representation at fixed resolution to improve path planning in cluttered environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-resolution mapping methods create overly conservative obstacle representations, leading to suboptimal paths or planning failures in cluttered scenes. There's a need for mapping that maximizes free space while maintaining map fidelity and planner compatibility.

Method: Parallel OctoMapping (POMP) - an efficient OctoMap-based technique that refines free space representation at fixed occupancy-grid resolution, supports multi-threaded computation, and preserves compatibility with existing search-based planners.

Result: POMP achieves higher pathfinding success rates, shorter path lengths (especially in cluttered environments), and substantially improved computational efficiency compared to traditional fixed-resolution methods.

Conclusion: POMP successfully addresses the limitations of fixed-resolution mapping by providing more accurate free space representation while maintaining planner compatibility, enabling better path planning performance in complex environments.

Abstract: Mapping is essential in robotics and autonomous systems because it provides the spatial foundation for path planning. Efficient mapping enables planning algorithms to generate reliable paths while ensuring safety and adapting in real time to complex environments. Fixed-resolution mapping methods often produce overly conservative obstacle representations that lead to suboptimal paths or planning failures in cluttered scenes. To address this issue, we introduce Parallel OctoMapping (POMP), an efficient OctoMap-based mapping technique that maximizes available free space and supports multi-threaded computation. To the best of our knowledge, POMP is the first method that, at a fixed occupancy-grid resolution, refines the representation of free space while preserving map fidelity and compatibility with existing search-based planners. It can therefore be integrated into existing planning pipelines, yielding higher pathfinding success rates and shorter path lengths, especially in cluttered environments, while substantially improving computational efficiency.

</details>


### [332] [Learning Sidewalk Autopilot from Multi-Scale Imitation with Corrective Behavior Expansion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22527)
*Honglin He,Yukai Ma,Brad Squicciarini,Wayne Wu,Bolei Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework that enhances imitation learning for sidewalk micromobility control through corrective behavior expansion and multi-scale learning, improving robustness and generalization in complex urban environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based control methods for sidewalk micromobility struggle in complex urban environments. Imitation learning from human demonstrations suffers from compounding errors, limited robustness, and poor generalization due to reliance on fixed offline data.

Method: Two-pronged approach: 1) Data augmentation with diverse corrective behaviors and sensor augmentations to enable policy recovery from mistakes, 2) Multi-scale IL architecture using horizon-based trajectory clustering and hierarchical supervision to capture both short-horizon interactive behaviors and long-horizon goal-directed intentions.

Result: Real-world experiments demonstrate significant improvements in robustness and generalization across diverse sidewalk scenarios compared to baseline imitation learning approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses key limitations of imitation learning for sidewalk micromobility control by combining data augmentation with corrective behaviors and multi-scale learning architecture, enabling more robust and generalizable performance in complex urban environments.

Abstract: Sidewalk micromobility is a promising solution for last-mile transportation, but current learning-based control methods struggle in complex urban environments. Imitation learning (IL) learns policies from human demonstrations, yet its reliance on fixed offline data often leads to compounding errors, limited robustness, and poor generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a framework that advances IL through corrective behavior expansion and multi-scale imitation learning. On the data side, we augment teleoperation datasets with diverse corrective behaviors and sensor augmentations to enable the policy to learn to recover from its own mistakes. On the model side, we introduce a multi-scale IL architecture that captures both short-horizon interactive behaviors and long-horizon goal-directed intentions via horizon-based trajectory clustering and hierarchical supervision. Real-world experiments show that our approach significantly improves robustness and generalization in diverse sidewalk scenarios.

</details>


### [333] [Allometric Scaling Laws for Bipedal Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22560)
*Naomi Oke,Aja Carter,Ben Gu,Steven Man,Cordelia Pride,Sarah Bergbreiter,Aaron M. Johnson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper establishes allometric scaling laws for bipedal robots across three orders of magnitude in leg length, revealing that robot mass scales with L² (not L³ as in isometric scaling), velocity follows L¹/², and torque scales with m*L rather than m*L².


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While biological systems follow established scaling laws (isometric and allometric), it's unclear how these relationships translate to robotic systems. Scaling robot designs up or down remains a fundamental challenge in robotics.

Method: 1) Literature review of legged robots to extract empirical relationships between leg length, body length, mass, and speed. 2) Controlled simulation studies in Drake using three variants of real quasi-passive, hip-actuated walkers with different foot geometries and control strategies, evaluating performance as designs are scaled with leg length.

Result: Robot mass scales more closely to L² (vs. L³ in isometric scaling). Walking velocity follows L¹/² trend from dynamic similarity. Minimum required torque scales more closely with m*L (vs. m*L² in isometric model). Foot geometry scales proportionally with L¹.

Conclusion: The study provides new insights into how robot designs allometrically scale to different sizes, revealing that robotic scaling laws differ from both isometric scaling and biological scaling laws, offering important design principles for scaling bipedal robots.

Abstract: Scaling the design of robots up or down remains a fundamental challenge. While biological systems follow well-established isometric and allometric scaling laws relating mass, stride frequency, velocity, and torque, it is unclear how these relationships translate to robotic systems. In this paper, we generate similar allometric scaling laws for bipedal robots across three orders of magnitude in leg length. First, we conduct a review of legged robots from the literature and extract empirical relationships between leg length (L), body length, mass, and speed. These data show that robot mass scales more closely to L^2, in contrast to the L^3 scaling predicted by isometric scaling. We then perform controlled simulation studies in Drake using three variants of real quasi-passive, hip-actuated walkers with different foot geometries and control strategies. We evaluate the performance of each design scaled with leg length, L. Across all robots, walking velocity follows the expected L^(1/2) trend from dynamic similarity. Minimum required torque scales more closely with m*L than the isometric model of m*L^2. Foot geometry scaled proportionally with L^1. These results provide new insight into how robot designs allometrically scale to different sizes, and how that scaling is different from isometric or biological scaling laws.

</details>


### [334] [GIFT: Generalizing Intent for Flexible Test-Time Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22574)
*Fin Amin,Nathaniel Dennler,Andreea Bobu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GIFT is a framework that uses language models to infer human intent from demonstrations, enabling learned reward functions to generalize to new environments without retraining by mapping test states to behaviorally equivalent training states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reward learning methods fail to generalize to new environments because they latch onto spurious correlations in training data rather than capturing the underlying human intent that demonstrations represent. Visual or semantic similarity cues often diverge from what humans actually care about.

Method: GIFT uses language models to infer high-level intent from user demonstrations by contrasting preferred with non-preferred behaviors. At deployment, it maps novel test states to behaviorally equivalent training states via intent-conditioned similarity, enabling reward generalization across distribution shifts without retraining.

Result: GIFT outperforms visual and semantic similarity baselines on tabletop manipulation tasks with new objects and layouts, achieving higher test-time pairwise win rates and state-alignment F1 scores across four simulated tasks with over 50 unseen objects. Real-world experiments on a 7-DoF Franka Panda robot demonstrate reliable transfer to physical settings.

Conclusion: GIFT successfully grounds reward generalization in human intent rather than surface cues, enabling robust transfer of learned rewards to novel environments without requiring retraining, with demonstrated effectiveness in both simulation and real-world robotic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Robots learn reward functions from user demonstrations, but these rewards often fail to generalize to new environments. This failure occurs because learned rewards latch onto spurious correlations in training data rather than the underlying human intent that demonstrations represent. Existing methods leverage visual or semantic similarity to improve robustness, yet these surface-level cues often diverge from what humans actually care about. We present Generalizing Intent for Flexible Test-Time Rewards (GIFT), a framework that grounds reward generalization in human intent rather than surface cues. GIFT leverages language models to infer high-level intent from user demonstrations by contrasting preferred with non-preferred behaviors. At deployment, GIFT maps novel test states to behaviorally equivalent training states via intent-conditioned similarity, enabling learned rewards to generalize across distribution shifts without retraining. We evaluate GIFT on tabletop manipulation tasks with new objects and layouts. Across four simulated tasks with over 50 unseen objects, GIFT consistently outperforms visual and semantic similarity baselines in test-time pairwise win rate and state-alignment F1 score. Real-world experiments on a 7-DoF Franka Panda robot demonstrate that GIFT reliably transfers to physical settings. Further discussion can be found at https://mit-clear-lab.github.io/GIFT/

</details>


### [335] [Task-Agnostic Exoskeleton Control Supports Elderly Joint Energetics during Hip-Intensive Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22580)
*Jiefu Zhang,Nikhil V. Divekar,Chandramouli Krishnan,Robert D. Gregg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hip exoskeleton with power-sensitive control reduces biological work by 24.7% at the hip and 9.3% overall during various mobility tasks in older adults, while augmenting total hip power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Older adults experience mobility decline with reduced ankle function compensated by increased hip demand, but paradoxically also have reduced maximal hip power. Existing robotic exoskeletons have limited applicability to real-world mobility demands beyond steady-state walking.

Method: Implemented a task-agnostic hip exoskeleton controller sensitive to joint power, validated in 8 older adults across multiple hip-intensive activities: level walking, ramp ascent, stair climbing, and sit-to-stand transitions.

Result: Exoskeleton matched biological power profiles with high accuracy (mean cosine similarity 0.89). Reduced sagittal plane biological positive work by 24.7% at the hip and 9.3% for lower limb. Augmented peak total hip power while reducing peak biological hip power.

Conclusion: Hip exoskeletons can enhance endurance through biological work reduction and increase functional reserve through total power augmentation, serving as a promising biomechanical intervention to support older adults' mobility.

Abstract: Age-related mobility decline is frequently accompanied by a redistribution of joint kinetics, where older adults compensate for reduced ankle function by increasing demand on the hip. Paradoxically, this compensatory shift typically coincides with age-related reductions in maximal hip power. Although robotic exoskeletons can provide immediate energetic benefits, conventional control strategies have limited previous studies in this population to specific tasks such as steady-state walking, which do not fully reflect mobility demands in the home and community. Here, we implement a task-agnostic hip exoskeleton controller that is inherently sensitive to joint power and validate its efficacy in eight older adults. Across a battery of hip-intensive activities that included level walking, ramp ascent, stair climbing, and sit-to-stand transitions, the exoskeleton matched biological power profiles with high accuracy (mean cosine similarity 0.89). Assistance significantly reduced sagittal plane biological positive work by 24.7% at the hip and by 9.3% for the lower limb, while simultaneously augmenting peak total (biological + exoskeleton) hip power and reducing peak biological hip power. These results suggest that hip exoskeletons can potentially enhance endurance through biological work reduction, and increase functional reserve through total power augmentation, serving as a promising biomechanical intervention to support older adults' mobility.

</details>


### [336] [Trajectory Generation for Underactuated Soft Robot Manipulators using Discrete Elastic Rod Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22604)
*Beibei Liu,Akua K. Dickson,Ran Jing,Andrew P. Sabelhaus*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A control-oriented reformulation of Discrete Elastic Rod dynamics for soft robots enables generation of dynamically feasible trajectories that improve tracking performance over constant-curvature baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft robots are ideal for contact-rich tasks due to compliance, but this same property makes accurate modeling challenging. Existing approaches either provide physical accuracy but are computationally demanding (continuum rod models) or improve efficiency at the cost of modeling fidelity (reduced-order approximations). There's a need for models that capture arbitrary deformations while remaining computationally efficient and compatible with underactuation.

Method: Introduces a control-oriented reformulation of Discrete Elastic Rod (DER) dynamics for soft robots, yielding a control-affine representation while preserving certain first-principles force-deformation relationships. Also presents a method to generate trajectories with these dynamics.

Result: The generated trajectories are both dynamically feasible and consistent with underlying actuation assumptions. Experimental validation on a pneumatic soft robotic limb demonstrates consistently improved trajectory tracking performance over a constant-curvature-based baseline, particularly under complex actuation conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed control-oriented reformulation of DER dynamics provides a balanced approach that maintains physical accuracy while enabling efficient trajectory generation for soft robots, addressing the trade-off between modeling fidelity and computational efficiency that has limited previous approaches.

Abstract: Soft robots are well suited for contact-rich tasks due to their compliance, yet this property makes accurate and tractable modeling challenging. Planning motions with dynamically-feasible trajectories requires models that capture arbitrary deformations, remain computationally efficient, and are compatible with underactuation. However, existing approaches balance these properties unevenly: continuum rod models provide physical accuracy but are computationally demanding, while reduced-order approximations improve efficiency at the cost of modeling fidelity. To address this, our work introduces a control-oriented reformulation of Discrete Elastic Rod (DER) dynamics for soft robots, and a method to generate trajectories with these dynamics. The proposed formulation yields a control-affine representation while preserving certain first-principles force-deformation relationships. As a result, the generated trajectories are both dynamically feasible and consistent with the underlying actuation assumptions. We present our trajectory generation framework and validate it experimentally on a pneumatic soft robotic limb. Hardware results demonstrate consistently improved trajectory tracking performance over a constant-curvature-based baseline, particularly under complex actuation conditions.

</details>


### [337] [Variable-Resolution Virtual Maps for Autonomous Exploration with Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22667)
*Ye Li,Yewei Huang,Wenlong GaoZhang,Alberto Quattrini Li,Brendan Englot,Yuanchang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VRVM is a variable-resolution virtual map method using adaptive quadtrees and Gaussian landmarks for efficient USV exploration in GNSS-degraded near-shore waters, balancing exploration-exploitation tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: USV exploration in near-shore waters faces challenges: GNSS degradation, environment-induced localization uncertainty, limited computation. Existing virtual map methods have poor scaling with fixed-resolution discretizations and can overvalue feature-sparse regions, increasing SLAM failure risk.

Method: Variable-Resolution Virtual Map (VRVM) using bivariate Gaussian virtual landmarks placed in adaptive quadtree cells. Adaptive quadtree enables area-weighted uncertainty representation (coarse far-field, high-resolution information-dense regions). EM planner evaluates pose and map uncertainty along frontiers to balance exploration-exploitation.

Result: VRVM outperforms state-of-the-art exploration algorithms in VRX Gazebo simulator with realistic marina environments across increasing difficulty scenarios. Offers safer behavior and better computational utilization in GNSS-degraded near-shore environments.

Conclusion: VRVM provides computationally efficient map uncertainty representation for USV exploration, addressing scaling limitations of fixed-resolution methods while balancing exploration-exploitation tradeoffs through adaptive resolution and EM planning.

Abstract: Autonomous exploration by unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) in near-shore waters requires reliable localisation and consistent mapping over extended areas, but this is challenged by GNSS degradation, environment-induced localisation uncertainty, and limited on-board computation. Virtual map-based methods explicitly model localisation and mapping uncertainty by tightly coupling factor-graph SLAM with a map uncertainty criterion. However, their storage and computational costs scale poorly with fixed-resolution workspace discretisations, leading to inefficiency in large near-shore environments. Moreover, overvaluing feature-sparse open-water regions can increase the risk of SLAM failure as a result of imbalance between exploration and exploitation. To address these limitations, we propose a Variable-Resolution Virtual Map (VRVM), a computationally efficient method for representing map uncertainty using bivariate Gaussian virtual landmarks placed in the cells of an adaptive quadtree. The adaptive quadtree enables an area-weighted uncertainty representation that keeps coarse, far-field virtual landmarks deliberately uncertain while allocating higher resolution to information-dense regions, and reduces the sensitivity of the map valuation to local refinements of the tree. An expectation-maximisation (EM) planner is adopted to evaluate pose and map uncertainty along frontiers using the VRVM, balancing exploration and exploitation. We evaluate VRVM against several state-of-the-art exploration algorithms in the VRX Gazebo simulator, using a realistic marina environment across different testing scenarios with an increasing level of exploration difficulty. The results indicate that our method offers safer behaviour and better utilisation of on-board computation in GNSS-degraded near-shore environments.

</details>


### [338] [Learning Safe-Stoppability Monitors for Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22703)
*Yifan Sun,Yiyuan Pan,Shangtao Li,Caiwu Ding,Tao Cui,Lingyun Wang,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PRISM framework uses neural predictors and importance sampling to identify safe-stoppable states for humanoid robots during emergency stops, enabling proactive safety monitoring with sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional emergency stop mechanisms that abruptly cut power can cause catastrophic failures in humanoid robots. Instead, robots need predefined fallback controllers that preserve balance and drive toward minimum-risk conditions, requiring identification of states from which safe emergency stops can be executed.

Method: Formalize emergency stopping as policy-dependent safe-stoppability problem. Introduce PRISM (Proactive Refinement of Importance-sampled Stoppability Monitor) - a simulation-driven framework that learns neural predictors for state-level stoppability. Uses importance sampling to iteratively refine decision boundaries with targeted exploration of rare but safety-critical states.

Result: PRISM significantly improves data efficiency while reducing false-safe predictions under fixed simulation budget. Successfully demonstrates sim-to-real transfer by deploying pretrained monitor on real humanoid platform. Enables proactive safety monitoring and scalable certification of fail-safe behaviors.

Conclusion: Modeling safety as policy-dependent stoppability provides effective framework for humanoid robot safety. PRISM's data-driven approach with targeted exploration enables practical safety certification and proactive monitoring for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Emergency stop (E-stop) mechanisms are the de facto standard for robot safety. However, for humanoid robots, abruptly cutting power can itself cause catastrophic failures; instead, an emergency stop must execute a predefined fallback controller that preserves balance and drives the robot toward a minimum-risk condition. This raises a critical question: from which states can a humanoid robot safely execute such a stop?
  In this work, we formalize emergency stopping for humanoids as a policy-dependent safe-stoppability problem and use data-driven approaches to characterize the safe-stoppable envelope. We introduce PRISM (Proactive Refinement of Importance-sampled Stoppability Monitor), a simulation-driven framework that learns a neural predictor for state-level stoppability. PRISM iteratively refines the decision boundary using importance sampling, enabling targeted exploration of rare but safety-critical states. This targeted exploration significantly improves data efficiency while reducing false-safe predictions under a fixed simulation budget. We further demonstrate sim-to-real transfer by deploying the pretrained monitor on a real humanoid platform. Results show that modeling safety as policy-dependent stoppability enables proactive safety monitoring and supports scalable certification of fail-safe behaviors for humanoid robots.

</details>


### [339] [Fleet-Level Battery-Health-Aware Scheduling for Autonomous Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22731)
*Jiachen Li,Shihao Li,Jian Chu,Wei Li,Dongmei Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Extends battery degradation-aware task planning from single to multi-robot fleets, jointly optimizing task assignment, sequencing, charging decisions, and charger access while balancing degradation across robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most battery-aware planning methods only address single robots, but autonomous mobile robot fleets need to coordinate task allocation and charging under limited shared resources while managing battery degradation.

Method: Uses degradation proxies from battery aging literature, linearizes bilinear idle aging term via disaggregated piecewise McCormick formulation, tightens LP relaxation with instance-derived big M values, and employs hierarchical matheuristic with fleet-level master problem and robot-level subproblems.

Result: Systematic experiments compare against three baselines: rule-based nearest available dispatcher, energy-aware formulation without degradation modeling, and charger-unaware formulation ignoring shared charger capacity limits.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable multi-robot battery degradation-aware planning framework that jointly optimizes task allocation, charging, and charger access while balancing degradation across the fleet.

Abstract: Autonomous mobile robot fleets must coordinate task allocation and charging under limited shared resources, yet most battery aware planning methods address only a single robot. This paper extends degradation cost aware task planning to a multi robot setting by jointly optimizing task assignment, service sequencing, optional charging decisions, charging mode selection, and charger access while balancing degradation across the fleet. The formulation relies on reduced form degradation proxies grounded in the empirical battery aging literature, capturing both charging mode dependent wear and idle state of charge dependent aging; the bilinear idle aging term is linearized through a disaggregated piecewise McCormick formulation. Tight big M values derived from instance data strengthen the LP relaxation. To manage scalability, we propose a hierarchical matheuristic in which a fleet level master problem coordinates assignments, routes, and charger usage, while robot level subproblems whose integer part decomposes into trivially small independent partition selection problems compute route conditioned degradation schedules. Systematic experiments compare the proposed method against three baselines: a rule based nearest available dispatcher, an energy aware formulation that enforces battery feasibility without modeling degradation, and a charger unaware formulation that accounts for degradation but ignores shared charger capacity limits.

</details>


### [340] [SG-VLA: Learning Spatially-Grounded Vision-Language-Action Models for Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22760)
*Ruisen Tu,Arth Shukla,Sohyun Yoo,Xuanlin Li,Junxi Li,Jianwen Xie,Hao Su,Zhuowen Tu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Spatially-grounded VLA framework improves robot manipulation in homes via multi-modal inputs and auxiliary task co-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action models underperform in complex household environments; mobile manipulation requires reasoning about global scene layout, fine geometry, and high-dimensional actions that standard imitation learning can't handle.

Method: Uses multi-view RGB, depth cues, and temporal history for spatial understanding; co-trains auxiliary decoders to reconstruct interpretable intermediate signals (robot position, joint configurations, grasp affordances, target-object pose, segmentation masks) from shared visual-language features.

Result: Achieves consistent improvements across picking, placing, opening, and closing operations in home rearrangement tasks, substantially outperforming direct imitation learning.

Conclusion: Spatial grounding through auxiliary and multi-modal learning provides a strong direction for scaling VLA models toward general-purpose domestic robots.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models show promise for robotic control, yet performance in complex household environments remains sub-optimal. Mobile manipulation requires reasoning about global scene layout, fine-grained geometry, and high-dimensional continuous actions, making standard imitation learning insufficient. We introduce a framework for learning spatially-grounded VLA models that strengthens perception and representation through auxiliary task co-training and multi-modal input enhancement. Our method addresses the challenge of controlling a 13-dimensional action space involving coordinated base motion, arm articulation, and gripper actuation. To enrich spatial understanding, the model incorporates multi-view RGB observations, depth cues, and short temporal history, providing perspectives of both global scene structure and local manipulation context. To improve representation quality, we co-train auxiliary decoders that reconstruct interpretable intermediate signals - including global robot position, joint configurations, grasp affordances, target-object relative pose, and segmentation masks - from shared visual-language features. These objectives provide dense supervision that encourages the backbone to develop spatially grounded, manipulation-aware latent representations. Through extensive evaluation on home rearrangement tasks, our approach achieves consistent improvements across picking, placing, opening, and closing operations, substantially outperforming direct imitation learning. Our findings suggest that spatial grounding through auxiliary and multi-modal learning provides a strong direction for scaling VLA models toward general-purpose domestic robots.

</details>


### [341] [Instrument-Splatting++: Towards Controllable Surgical Instrument Digital Twin Using Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22792)
*Shuojue Yang,Zijian Wu,Chengjiaao Liao,Qian Li,Daiyun Shen,Chang Han Low,Septimiu E. Salcudean,Yueming Jin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Instrument-Splatting++ is a monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting framework that reconstructs surgical instruments as controllable Gaussian assets from unposed endoscopic videos, enabling realistic simulation and synthetic data generation for robot-assisted surgery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality and controllable digital twins of surgical instruments are critical for Real2Sim in robot-assisted surgery, as they enable realistic simulation, synthetic data generation, and perception learning under novel poses.

Method: The framework uses part-wise geometry pretraining with CAD priors, semantics-aware pose estimation and tracking (SAPET) to recover 6-DoF poses and joint angles from videos, and robust texture learning (RTL) that alternates pose refinement and appearance optimization.

Result: The method shows superior photometric quality and improved geometric accuracy over state-of-the-art baselines on EndoVis17/18, SAR-RARP, and in-house datasets, and demonstrates improved performance in downstream keypoint detection tasks through unseen-pose data augmentation.

Conclusion: Instrument-Splatting++ successfully creates controllable digital twins of surgical instruments from monocular videos, advancing Real2Sim capabilities for robot-assisted surgery with applications in simulation, synthetic data generation, and perception learning.

Abstract: High-quality and controllable digital twins of surgical instruments are critical for Real2Sim in robot-assisted surgery, as they enable realistic simulation, synthetic data generation, and perception learning under novel poses. We present Instrument-Splatting++, a monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework that reconstructs surgical instruments as a fully controllable Gaussian asset with high fidelity. Our pipeline starts with part-wise geometry pretraining that injects CAD priors into Gaussian primitives and equips the representation with part-aware semantic rendering. Built on the pretrained model, we propose a semantics-aware pose estimation and tracking (SAPET) method to recover per-frame 6-DoF pose and joint angles from unposed endoscopic videos, where a gripper-tip network trained purely from synthetic semantics provides robust supervision and a loose regularization suppresses singular articulations. Finally, we introduce Robust Texture Learning (RTL), which alternates pose refinement and robust appearance optimization, mitigating pose noise during texture learning. The proposed framework can perform pose estimation and learn realistic texture from unposed videos. We validate our method on sequences extracted from EndoVis17/18, SAR-RARP, and an in-house dataset, showing superior photometric quality and improved geometric accuracy over state-of-the-art baselines. We further demonstrate a downstream keypoint detection task where unseen-pose data augmentation from our controllable instrument Gaussian improves performance.

</details>


### [342] [CATNAV: Cached Vision-Language Traversability for Efficient Zero-Shot Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22800)
*Aditya Potnis,Francisco Affonso,Shreya Gummadi,Naveen Kumar Uppalapati,Girish Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CATNAV is a zero-shot navigation framework that uses multimodal LLMs to generate embodiment-aware costmaps for robots in unstructured environments, with visuosemantic caching to reduce VLM queries by 85.7% and trajectory selection for safer path planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current navigation systems struggle with assessing traversal risk in unstructured environments that varies across different robot embodiments, requiring task-specific training and lacking efficient risk assessment mechanisms.

Method: CATNAV uses multimodal LLMs for zero-shot, embodiment-aware costmap generation without training. It includes visuosemantic caching to detect scene novelty and reuse prior risk assessments for similar frames, plus VLM-based trajectory selection that evaluates proposals through visual reasoning.

Result: On a quadruped robot across indoor/outdoor unstructured environments, CATNAV achieved 10 percentage point higher average goal-reaching rate and 33% fewer behavioral constraint violations compared to state-of-the-art vision-language-action baselines across five navigation tasks.

Conclusion: CATNAV demonstrates effective zero-shot navigation in unstructured environments by combining multimodal LLMs for embodiment-aware risk assessment with caching for efficiency and visual reasoning for safer trajectory selection.

Abstract: Navigating unstructured environments requires assessing traversal risk relative to a robot's physical capabilities, a challenge that varies across embodiments. We present CATNAV, a cost-aware traversability navigation framework that leverages multimodal LLMs for zero-shot, embodiment-aware costmap generation without task-specific training. We introduce a visuosemantic caching mechanism that detects scene novelty and reuses prior risk assessments for semantically similar frames, reducing online VLM queries by 85.7%. Furthermore, we introduce a VLM-based trajectory selection module that evaluates proposals through visual reasoning to choose the safest path given behavioral constraints. We evaluate CATNAV on a quadruped robot across indoor and outdoor unstructured environments, comparing against state-of-the-art vision-language-action baselines. Across five navigation tasks, CATNAV achieves 10 percentage point higher average goal-reaching rate and 33% fewer behavioral constraint violations.

</details>


### [343] [DecompGrind: A Decomposition Framework for Robotic Grinding via Cutting-Surface Planning and Contact-Force Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22859)
*Shunsuke Araki,Takumi Hachimine,Yuki Saito,Kouhei Ohnishi,Jun Morimoto,Takamitsu Masubara*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DecompGrind decomposes robotic grinding into removal-shape planning and contact-force adaptation, combining geometric planning with imitation learning for efficient grinding of diverse shapes and materials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic grinding automation is challenging due to varying removal resistance with different shapes and material hardness. Traditional approaches struggle with accurate resistance estimation and shape modeling, while learning methods require large training datasets for diverse conditions.

Method: DecompGrind framework with two components: 1) Global Cutting-Surface Planning (GCSP) - determines removal shapes through geometric analysis without learning, 2) Local Contact-Force Adaptation (LCFA) - learns contact-force adaptation policy using bilateral control-based imitation learning during grinding of each removal shape.

Result: Experiments with robotic grinding system and 3D-printed workpieces demonstrate efficient grinding of workpieces with different shapes and material hardness while maintaining safe contact force levels.

Conclusion: The decomposition approach restricts learning to local contact-force adaptation, enabling policy learning from few demonstrations while handling global shape transition geometrically, addressing challenges in robotic grinding automation.

Abstract: Robotic grinding is widely used for shaping workpieces in manufacturing, but it remains difficult to automate this process efficiently. In particular, efficiently grinding workpieces of different shapes and material hardness is challenging because removal resistance varies with local contact conditions. Moreover, it is difficult to achieve accurate estimation of removal resistance and analytical modeling of shape transition, and learning-based approaches often require large amounts of training data to cover diverse processing conditions. To address these challenges, we decompose robotic grinding into two components: removal-shape planning and contact-force adaptation. Based on this formulation, we propose DecompGrind, a framework that combines Global Cutting-Surface Planning (GCSP) and Local Contact-Force Adaptation (LCFA). GCSP determines removal shapes through geometric analysis of the current and target shapes without learning, while LCFA learns a contact-force adaptation policy using bilateral control-based imitation learning during the grinding of each removal shape. This decomposition restricts learning to local contact-force adaptation, allowing the policy to be learned from a small number of demonstrations, while handling global shape transition geometrically. Experiments using a robotic grinding system and 3D-printed workpieces demonstrate efficient robotic grinding of workpieces having different shapes and material hardness while maintaining safe levels of contact force.

</details>


### [344] [Grounding Sim-to-Real Generalization in Dexterous Manipulation: An Empirical Study with Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22876)
*Ruixing Jin,Zicheng Zhu,Ruixiang Ouyang,Sheng Xu,Bo Yue,Zhizheng Wu,Guiliang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper empirically examines Sim-to-Real generalization for dexterous manipulation policies, evaluating four key factors across 10k+ real-world trials and releasing a standardized benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data collection for dexterous manipulation is expensive, so synthetic simulation data is used, but it suffers from Sim-to-Real distribution gaps. While many algorithms address this, there's a lack of principled research on how these methods perform on generalist policies like Vision-Language-Action models in real-world manipulation tasks.

Method: The study empirically examines four dimensions of Sim-to-Real generalization: multi-level domain randomization, photorealistic rendering, physics-realistic modeling, and reinforcement learning updates. They design a comprehensive evaluation protocol accounting for background, lighting, distractors, object types, and spatial features variations. Experiments involve over 10,000 real-world trials.

Result: Through extensive experiments, the researchers derive critical insights into Sim-to-Real transfer. They release both robotic platforms and the evaluation protocol publicly to facilitate independent verification and establish a realistic, standardized benchmark for dexterous manipulation policies.

Conclusion: The study provides empirical analysis of Sim-to-Real generalization factors and establishes a standardized benchmark to advance future research in dexterous manipulation, addressing the gap between simulation-based training and real-world performance of generalist control policies.

Abstract: Learning a generalist control policy for dexterous manipulation typically relies on large-scale datasets. Given the high cost of real-world data collection, a practical alternative is to generate synthetic data through simulation. However, the resulting synthetic data often exhibits a significant gap from real-world distributions. While many prior studies have proposed algorithms to bridge the Sim-to-Real discrepancy, there remains a lack of principled research that grounds these methods in real-world manipulation tasks, particularly their performance on generalist policies such as Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. In this study, we empirically examine the primary determinants of Sim-to-Real generalization across four dimensions: multi-level domain randomization, photorealistic rendering, physics-realistic modeling, and reinforcement learning updates. To support this study, we design a comprehensive evaluation protocol to quantify the real-world performance of manipulation tasks. The protocol accounts for key variations in background, lighting, distractors, object types, and spatial features. Through experiments involving over 10k real-world trials, we derive critical insights into Sim-to-Real transfer. To inform and advance future studies, we release both the robotic platforms and the evaluation protocol for public access to facilitate independent verification, thereby establishing a realistic and standardized benchmark for dexterous manipulation policies.

</details>


### [345] [Agile-VLA: Few-Shot Industrial Pose Rectification via Implicit Affordance Anchoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22899)
*Teng Yan,Zhengyang Pei,Chengyu Shi,Yue Yu,Yikun Chen,Zilong Zhu,Zelin Fang,Kaile Guo,Zihang Wang,Peigen Tian,Bingzhuo Zhong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Agile-VLA is a hierarchical framework for edge devices that uses Implicit Affordance Anchoring to map geometric visual cues directly to action primitives, reducing semantic inference latency while enabling robust 6-DoF manipulation with minimal demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a fundamental conflict between high-latency semantic inference in Vision-Language-Action models and the high-frequency control needed for dynamic manipulation on resource-constrained edge platforms like NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano.

Method: Uses Implicit Affordance Anchoring to map geometric visual cues (centroid and rim keypoint anchors) directly to structured parametric action primitives. Features an asynchronous dual-stream architecture that decouples perception (10 Hz) from control (50 Hz) to address frequency mismatch.

Result: Demonstrates robust rectification of complex, irregular workpieces using only 5-shot demonstrations through extrinsic dexterity on a standard 6-DoF manipulator.

Conclusion: Agile-VLA effectively addresses the latency-frequency conflict in edge-based robot learning by reducing reliance on high-latency semantic inference while maintaining high-frequency control for dynamic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Deploying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on resource-constrained edge platforms encounters a fundamental conflict between high-latency semantic inference and the high-frequency control required for dynamic manipulation. To address the challenge, this paper presents Agile-VLA, a hierarchical framework designed for industrial pose reorientation tasks on edge devices such as the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano. The core innovation is an Implicit Affordance Anchoring mechanism that directly maps geometric visual cues, specifically centroid and rim keypoint anchors, into structured parametric action primitives, thereby substantially reducing reliance on high-latency semantic inference during closed-loop control. By decoupling perception (10 Hz) from control (50 Hz) via an asynchronous dual-stream architecture, the system effectively mitigates the frequency mismatch inherent in edge-based robot learning. Experimental results on a standard 6-DoF manipulator demonstrate that Agile-VLA achieves robust rectification of complex, irregular workpieces using only 5-shot demonstrations through extrinsic dexterity.

</details>


### [346] [Task-Aware Positioning for Improvisational Tasks in Mobile Construction Robots via an AI Agent with Multi-LMM Modules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22903)
*Seongju Jang,Francis Baek,SangHyun Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An LMM-based agent enables mobile construction robots to handle improvisational tasks by interpreting natural language instructions, navigating via drawings, and visually identifying task locations, achieving 92.2% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Construction sites involve many improvisational tasks where locations, timing, and contextual information are not known in advance. Existing mobile construction robots lack the capability to handle such non-predefined tasks.

Method: Proposed an agent with three parallel LMM modules: 1) task interpretation and breakdown from natural language, 2) construction drawing-based navigation, and 3) visual reasoning to identify non-predefined task locations. Implemented on a quadruped robot.

Result: Achieved 92.2% success rate for identifying and positioning at task-required locations across three tests designed to assess improvisational task handling.

Conclusion: The study enables mobile construction robots to perform non-predefined tasks autonomously by combining LMMs for task understanding, navigation, and visual reasoning in improvisational construction environments.

Abstract: Due to the ever-changing nature of construction, many tasks on sites occur in an improvisational manner. Existing mobile construction robot studies remain limited in addressing improvisational tasks, where task-required locations, timing of task occurrence, and contextual information required for task execution are not known in advance. We propose an agent that understands improvisational tasks given in natural language, identifies the task-required location, and positions itself. The agent's functionality was decomposed into three Large Multimodal Model (LMM) modules operating in parallel, enabling the application of LMMs for task interpretation and breakdown, construction drawing-based navigation, and visual reasoning to identify non-predefined task-required locations. The agent was implemented with a quadruped robot and achieved a 92.2% success rate for identifying and positioning at task-required locations across three tests designed to assess improvisational task handling. This study enables mobile construction robots to perform non-predefined tasks autonomously.

</details>


### [347] [Learning Actuator-Aware Spectral Submanifolds for Precise Control of Continuum Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23044)
*Paul Leonard Wolff,Hugo Buurmeijer,Luis Pabon,John Irvin Alora,Mark Leone,Roshan S. Kaundinya,Amirhossein Kazemipour,Robert K. Katzschmann,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Control-augmented SSMs (caSSMs) extend spectral submanifold reduction by incorporating control inputs into state representation, enabling real-time control of continuum robots with 40% lower prediction error and 52% lower tracking error compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuum robots have high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamics coupled with actuation mechanisms. Existing SSM reduction methods for control require additional actuation-calibration steps, creating complexity in modeling and control of these systems.

Method: Proposed control-augmented SSMs (caSSMs) explicitly incorporate control inputs into the state representation to capture nonlinear state-input couplings. Models are trained solely on controlled decay trajectories of actuator-augmented states, eliminating the need for separate actuation-calibration steps.

Result: Applied to a tendon-driven trunk robot, caSSM achieves 40% reduction in open-loop prediction error compared to existing methods. In closed-loop experiments with MPC, caSSM reduces tracking error by 52% compared to Koopman and SSM-based MPC approaches.

Conclusion: caSSMs provide an effective framework for real-time control of continuum robots by integrating control inputs directly into SSM reduction, demonstrating practical deployability on hardware continuum robots with significantly improved performance over existing methods.

Abstract: Continuum robots exhibit high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamics which are often coupled with their actuation mechanism. Spectral submanifold (SSM) reduction has emerged as a leading method for reducing high-dimensional nonlinear dynamical systems to low-dimensional invariant manifolds. Our proposed control-augmented SSMs (caSSMs) extend this methodology by explicitly incorporating control inputs into the state representation, enabling these models to capture nonlinear state-input couplings. Training these models relies solely on controlled decay trajectories of the actuator-augmented state, thereby removing the additional actuation-calibration step commonly needed by prior SSM-for-control methods. We learn a compact caSSM model for a tendon-driven trunk robot, enabling real-time control and reducing open-loop prediction error by 40% compared to existing methods. In closed-loop experiments with model predictive control (MPC), caSSM reduces tracking error by 52%, demonstrating improved performance against Koopman and SSM based MPC and practical deployability on hardware continuum robots.

</details>


### [348] [Tightly-Coupled Radar-Visual-Inertial Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23052)
*Morten Nissov,Mohit Singh,Kostas Alexis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a tightly coupled Visual-Inertial-Radar Odometry (VIRVO) system that fuses camera, radar, and IMU data for robust state estimation in challenging environments where visual-only or radar-only methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VIO works well in normal conditions but struggles in dark, low-texture, or obscured environments. RIO offers robustness to visual challenges but has reduced information density and worse long-term accuracy. The authors aim to combine both modalities to create a system that operates robustly regardless of environmental conditions or trajectory dynamics.

Method: The proposed method tightly couples visual features, radar Doppler measurements, and IMU data within an Iterated Extended Kalman Filter (IEKF) in real-time. Radar range data is used to augment visual feature depth initialization, creating a synergistic fusion of all three sensor modalities.

Result: The method was evaluated through flight experiments in both indoor and outdoor environments, and tested under challenging conditions for both exteroceptive modalities (darkness, fog, fast flight). The results thoroughly demonstrate the system's robustness across diverse operating conditions.

Conclusion: By tightly coupling visual, radar, and inertial measurements, the proposed system achieves robust state estimation that overcomes the limitations of individual sensor modalities, enabling reliable operation in a wide range of challenging environmental conditions and flight dynamics.

Abstract: Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) is a staple for reliable state estimation on constrained and lightweight platforms due to its versatility and demonstrated performance. However, pertinent challenges regarding robust operation in dark, low-texture, obscured environments complicate the use of such methods. Alternatively, Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radars, and by extension Radar-Inertial Odometry (RIO), offer robustness to these visual challenges, albeit at the cost of reduced information density and worse long-term accuracy. To address these limitations, this work combines the two in a tightly coupled manner, enabling the resulting method to operate robustly regardless of environmental conditions or trajectory dynamics. The proposed method fuses image features, radar Doppler measurements, and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) measurements within an Iterated Extended Kalman Filter (IEKF) in real-time, with radar range data augmenting the visual feature depth initialization. The method is evaluated through flight experiments conducted in both indoor and outdoor environments, as well as through challenges to both exteroceptive modalities (such as darkness, fog, or fast flight), thoroughly demonstrating its robustness. The implementation of the proposed method is available at: https://github.com/ntnu-arl/radvio .

</details>


### [349] [AirSimAG: A High-Fidelity Simulation Platform for Air-Ground Collaborative Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23079)
*Yangjie Cui,Xin Dong,Boyang Gao,Jinwu Xiang,Daochun Li,Zhan Tu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AirsimAG is a high-fidelity simulation platform for air-ground collaborative systems (UAVs and UGVs) built on customized AirSim, enabling synchronized multi-agent simulation with heterogeneous sensing and control interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing simulation platforms are designed for single-agent dynamics and lack dedicated frameworks for interactive air-ground collaborative simulation needed for complex applications like search and rescue, urban surveillance, and environmental monitoring.

Method: Developed AirsimAG by extensively customizing the AirSim framework to support synchronized multi-agent simulation with heterogeneous sensing and control interfaces for UAV-UGV systems.

Result: Created representative air-ground collaborative tasks (mapping, planning, tracking, formation, exploration) and provided quantitative analyses demonstrating platform effectiveness in supporting multi-agent coordination and cross-modal data consistency.

Conclusion: AirsimAG successfully addresses the gap in air-ground collaborative simulation platforms and is publicly available as an open-source tool for research and development in heterogeneous multi-agent systems.

Abstract: As spatial intelligence continues to evolve, heterogeneous multi-agent systems-particularly the collaboration between Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs), have demonstrated strong potential in complex applications such as search and rescue, urban surveillance, and environmental monitoring. However, existing simulation platforms are primarily designed for single-agent dynamics and lack dedicated frameworks for interactive air-ground collaborative simulation. In this paper, we present AirsimAG, a high-fidelity air-ground collaborative simulation platform built upon an extensively customized AirSim framework. The platform enables synchronized multi-agent simulation and supports heterogeneous sensing and control interfaces for UAV-UGV systems. To demonstrate its capabilities, we design a set of representative air-ground collaborative tasks, including mapping, planning, tracking, formation, and exploration. We further provide quantitative analyses based on these tasks to illustrate the platform effectiveness in supporting multi-agent coordination and cross-modal data consistency. The AirsimAG simulation platform is publicly available at https://github.com/BIULab-BUAA/AirSimAG.

</details>


### [350] [Active Robotic Perception for Disease Detection and Mapping in Apple Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23112)
*Hayden Feddock,Francisco Yandun,Srđan Aćimović,Abhisesh Silwal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autonomous mobile system for targeted disease detection in apple trees using active perception with semantic 3D mapping and viewpoint planning strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual disease monitoring in large-scale orchards is labor-intensive and impractical, leading to late detection at coarse resolutions. Need for precise, automated disease detection systems.

Method: Integrates flash-illuminated stereo RGB sensing, real-time depth estimation, instance-level segmentation, and confidence-aware semantic 3D mapping. Evaluates three viewpoint planning strategies: deterministic geometric baseline, volumetric NBV planner (maximizes unknown-space reduction), and semantic NBV planner (prioritizes low-confidence symptomatic regions).

Result: In simulation: semantic planner achieved highest F1 score (0.6106) after 30 viewpoints; volumetric planner achieved highest ROI coverage (85.82%). In lab setting: semantic planner attained highest final F1 (0.9058), with both NBV planners substantially improving coverage over baseline.

Conclusion: Autonomous active perception system enables precise disease localization and mapping for orchard management. Semantic viewpoint planning shows superior performance for targeted disease detection, providing actionable spatial maps for growers.

Abstract: Large-scale orchard production requires timely and precise disease monitoring, yet routine manual scouting is labor-intensive and financially impractical at the scale of modern operations. As a result, disease outbreaks are often detected late and tracked at coarse spatial resolutions, typically at the orchard-block level. We present an autonomous mobile active perception system for targeted disease detection and mapping in dormant apple trees, demonstrated on one of the most devastating diseases affecting apple today -- fire blight. The system integrates flash-illuminated stereo RGB sensing, real-time depth estimation, instance-level segmentation, and confidence-aware semantic 3D mapping to achieve precise localization of disease symptoms. Semantic predictions are fused into the volumetric occupancy map representation enabling the tracking of both occupancy and per-voxel semantic confidence, building actionable spatial maps for growers. To actively refine observations within complex canopies, we evaluate three viewpoint planning strategies within a unified perception-action loop: a deterministic geometric baseline, a volumetric next-best-view planner that maximizes unknown-space reduction, and a semantic next-best-view planner that prioritizes low-confidence symptomatic regions. Experiments on a fabricated lab tree and five simulated symptomatic trees demonstrate reliable symptom localization and mapping as a precursor to a field evaluation. In simulation, the semantic planner achieves the highest F1 score (0.6106) after 30 viewpoints, while the volumetric planner achieves the highest ROI coverage (85.82\%). In the lab setting, the semantic planner attains the highest final F1 (0.9058), with both next-best-view planners substantially improving coverage over the baseline.

</details>


### [351] [PHANTOM Hand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23152)
*Teng Yan,Jiongxu Chen,Qixiang Hua,Yue Yu,Zihang Wang,Yaohua Liu,Bingzhuo Zhong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PHANTOM Hand: A hybrid underactuated hand system that combines precise kinematic control with compliant grasping, achieving sub-degree accuracy while maintaining mechanical compliance for complex manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tendon-driven underactuated hands are good for adaptive grasping but suffer from kinematic unpredictability and non-linear force transmission, limiting precise free-motion shaping and reliable payload delivery for complex manipulation tasks.

Method: Developed PHANTOM Hand with 6 actuators and 15 DoFs, using a unified framework with sparse mapping from physical geometry and mechanics-based compensation model to suppress kinematic drift from spring counter-tension and tendon elasticity.

Result: Achieved sub-degree kinematic reproducibility, verified through kinematic analysis, static expressibility tests, diverse grasping experiments (power, precision, tool-use), and quantitative fingertip force characterization.

Conclusion: PHANTOM hand successfully combines analytic kinematic precision with continuous, predictable force output, expanding payload and dexterity of underactuated hands, with all hardware designs and control scripts open-sourced.

Abstract: Tendon-driven underactuated hands excel in adaptive grasping but often suffer from kinematic unpredictability and highly non-linear force transmission. This ambiguity limits their ability to perform precise free-motion shaping and deliver reliable payloads for complex manipulation tasks. To address this, we introduce the PHANTOM Hand (Hybrid Precision-Augmented Compliance): a modular, 1:1 human-scale system featuring 6 actuators and 15 degrees of freedom (DoFs).
  We propose a unified framework that bridges the gap between precise analytic shaping and robust compliant grasping. By deriving a sparse mapping from physical geometry and integrating a mechanics-based compensation model, we effectively suppress kinematic drift caused by spring counter-tension and tendon elasticity. This approach achieves sub-degree kinematic reproducibility for free-motion planning while retaining the inherent mechanical compliance required for stable physical interaction.
  Experimental validation confirms the system's capabilities through (1) kinematic analysis verifying sub-degree global accuracy across the workspace; (2) static expressibility tests demonstrating complex hand gestures; (3) diverse grasping experiments covering power, precision, and tool-use categories; and (4) quantitative fingertip force characterization. The results demonstrate that the PHANTOM hand successfully combines analytic kinematic precision with continuous, predictable force output, significantly expanding the payload and dexterity of underactuated hands. To drive the development of the underactuated manipulation ecosystem, all hardware designs and control scripts are fully open-sourced for community engagement.

</details>


### [352] [LiZIP: An Auto-Regressive Compression Framework for LiDAR Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.23162)
*Aditya Shibu,Kayvan Karim,Claudio Zito*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiZIP is a lightweight neural predictive coding framework for near-lossless LiDAR point cloud compression that achieves superior compression ratios compared to industry standards while maintaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Massive LiDAR data creates bottlenecks for real-time processing and V2X transmission in autonomous vehicles. Existing methods force trade-offs: industry standards lack adaptability, while deep learning approaches have prohibitive computational costs.

Method: LiZIP uses a lightweight neural predictive coding framework with a compact Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to predict point coordinates from local context, then efficiently encodes only the sparse residuals for near-lossless compression.

Result: LiZIP achieves 7.5%-14.8% reduction vs LASzip, 8.8%-11.3% reduction vs Google Draco, and 38%-48% reduction vs GZip. It also demonstrates generalization on unseen datasets without retraining.

Conclusion: LiZIP provides an efficient compression solution for bandwidth-constrained V2X applications and large-scale cloud archival of LiDAR data, balancing compression performance with computational efficiency.

Abstract: The massive volume of data generated by LiDAR sensors in autonomous vehicles creates a bottleneck for real-time processing and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) transmission. Existing lossless compression methods often force a trade-off: industry standard algorithms (e.g., LASzip) lack adaptability, while deep learning approaches suffer from prohibitive computational costs. This paper proposes LiZIP, a lightweight, near-lossless zero-drift compression framework based on neural predictive coding. By utilizing a compact Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to predict point coordinates from local context, LiZIP efficiently encodes only the sparse residuals.
  We evaluate LiZIP on the NuScenes and Argoverse datasets, benchmarking against GZip, LASzip, and Google Draco (configured with 24-bit quantization to serve as a high-precision geometric baseline).
